title: Angels
wikitext:
[[Image:OP angel sachiel face clean.jpg|thumb|right|"Angel face."]] '''Angel''' (使徒, ''shito'') is an ambiguous term that has various meanings within the ''Evangelion'' mythos depending on context. ==Usage== As most commonly used in the show and elsewhere, the word "Angel" refers to any of [[Adam]]'s [[Angel#Adam's Children|fifteen children]], whom Nerv must systematically destroy over the course of the story. Adam itself, the designated "First Angel," is occasionally included with its offspring when speaking of Angels collectively.'''[[Episode 12]]''':
* Misato: "Destroying Angels is my duty."
Ritsuko: "Your duty? Don't make me laugh. This is for your own sake, isn't it? Your revenge against the Angels!"
* Misato: "The only thing that was clear to me was I wanted to destroy the Angels who caused the Second Impact. So in order to do that, I joined Nerv." However, considering Seele's list of designated "Angels" in full, it includes entities as disparate as the [[Seed of Life|Seeds of Life]] (1st: [[Adam]] and 2nd: [[Lilith]]Lilith's designation as the Second Angel is never provided in-show, although it can be reasonably deduced due to the fact that no Second Angel is ever named, and Lilith is never assigned an ordinal. Its status as Second Angel is stated outright in several supplemental canonical sources (including [[Neon Genesis Evangelion: The Card Game|Bandai's collectible card game]], the [[Classified Information (Translation)|Classified Information]], and [[Evangelion Chronicle]]). It is also stated directly in the Rebuild canon:
:[[Evangelion 1.0: You Are (Not) Alone]]. Misato (showing Shinji the giant in Central Dogma):
:"[This is] the bringer of life to this planet, and the agent of its extinction. The Second Angel, Lilith."[[Episode 26']]. Misato: "Shinji-kun, we humans were born from the one called Lilith---a source of life, just like Adam. We are the 18th Angel." This translation stems from Bochan_bird's reading of the script, which is ambiguous as spoken; he apparently also ran the question [http://eva.onegeek.org/pipermail/evangelion/2004-May/001188.html by Tsurumaki in 2001].), the natural progeny of Adam (3rd~16th), the human-shaped vessel of Adam's soul (17th: [[Kaworu Nagisa]]), and ''Homo sapiens'' itself (18th: Lilin). This conflict between the heavily-reinforced concept of "Angel" (god-like beings antithetical to human life) and the more inclusive version rightfully raises the question of what an Angel is actually intended to be. ''Evangelion'' plays a similar game with terms related to [[human|humanity]] (''hito'', ''ningen'', and ''jinrui''). ===Analysis=== One possibility is that "Angel" actually refers to all beings whose origin is ultimately traceable to the [[First Ancestral Race]]. This species, the prototypical humans of NGE, produced the Seeds of Life in order to propagate new lifeforms on distant worlds, into which the F.A.R.'s souls would be reincarnated.Neon Genesis Evangelion 2: Another Cases. Kaworu's Scenario: Good Ending. Consider also that both "angel" and "apostle" (the literal meaning of Angel's Japanese equivalent, ''shito'') inherently mean "messenger." Furthermore, the F.A.R. are stated to be esteemed as gods by Adam's children and the Lilin.''[[Neon Genesis Evangelion 2]]''. [[Classified_Information_(Translation)#First_Ancestral_Race|Classified Information. First Ancestral Race.]] (On a perhaps related note, Rei muses, "What is a human? A creation of God? Is man a human creation?")''[[Neon Genesis Evangelion]]''. [[Episode 14]]. Platinum subtitles. "Angels", then – which we know to refer to both Seeds of Life and their progeny – could be thought of as "messengers" for their "God(s)." The "message" to be disseminated is humanity itself. ==Translation== {{expand}} The characters in NGE are actually calling the Angels "shito," which means "apostle," while the Japanese word normally used for the western concept of Angel is "tenshi." However, this isn't a translation mistake on the part of NGE's U.S. distributors. The "Angel" designation is clearly seen in the show's opening and at various times within the show itself, and the word "Angel" was retained in the English version of the show at the request of the show's Japanese creators{{fact}}. [[Neon Genesis Evangelion Proposal|At one point in the show's production]], the term "Apostolo" was going to be used as a counterpart to "shito" instead of "Angel.".Omori: Yes, it is. It also has challenges that seem to anticipate cyberpunk. However, Mitsuse's world is more oriental in its view of impermanence, but Evangelion is more Western.
Anno: I'm not into Western civilization, you know. Somehow I don't trust Western civilization very much.
Omori: Is that as something to be denied?
Anno: It doesn't relate to me, so I can use it. If I were a Christian, I'd be too scared to use Christian stuffs.
Omori: Indeed. You don't have any attachments, so you can use the name of an angel. Like let's use this name because its sense of language sounds nice.
Anno: To equate apostles and angels is so much to complain about from a westerner's point of view. There is an American worker in our company, and he scolded me for many things, saying it was wrong. That's normal. But I didn't care about that and just did it. - [https://wiki.evageeks.org/Statements_by_Evangelion_Staff#Hideaki_Anno:_The_World_of_Neon_Genesis_Evangelion_.28SF_Magazine_08.2F1996.29 The World of Neon Genesis Evangelion, SF Magazine, Aug 1996] ==Adam's Children== '''Adam’s children''' (アダムの子供達, ''Adamu no kodomotachi'')The collective grouping has no unique canonical name, although the phrase “Adam’s children” was used by [[Misato Katsuragi|Misato]] in a preliminary draft for [[Resources:End of Evangelion Screenplays|Episode 25’]]. refers to the [[Angels]] originated from the [[Seed of Life]] [[Adam]]. In the ''[[Neon Genesis Evangelion]]'' TV series, Adam's children encompass the Third through Seventeenth Angels (manga: Third through Twelfth). The first thirteen are Adam’s natural offspring (upon whom this article will focus). Adam’s last child, [[Kaworu Nagisa|Tabris]], is the result of an [[Contact Experiment|experiment]] in which [[Lilin]] genes fused into Adam. Adam's children began appearing around Tokyo-3 fifteen years after [[Second Impact]], starting with the 3rd Angel, Sachiel. It is assumed that the Angels' intention is to cause [[Third Impact]] and reclaim Earth from Lilith's children (''Homo sapiens''). The [[Evangelions]]' task is to protect mankind by defeating Adam's children, in accordance with both [[Gendo Ikari]]'s and Seele's plans. ===Nature=== {{NPOVAccuracy}} Despite being segregated into separate “Angels” (Third, Fourth, etc.), Adam’s children essentially comprise a single race of humanity – one possible variation upon a theme. (The Lilin are another.) They are described as “humans who cast aside human form.” Accordingly, they appear to begin life as humanoid entities, developing embryologically in perhaps the same way as Adam or an Eva would. Going by [[Sandalphon]], the Angels develop inside oblong objects resembling eggs, compared in the show to a chrysalis. After developing to an indeterminate pointIn episode 10, Ritsuko says that Sandalphon hatched sooner than expected, leaving the “norm” unknown., the Angel's human form is “rejected.” Immediately prior to hatching (again, going by Sandalphon's precedent), the Angel undergoes a spontaneous metamorphosis into its "adult form," then emerges from its "chrysalis." While the terms used invite an insect analogy, there is no canonical evidence that the Angels had a free-moving "pupal" stage at some point and later cocooned themselves. Rather, "chrysalis" seems to apply to the extent that the entity it contains undergoes a drastic metamorphosis before emerging. How the form is selected and its structure determined is entirely unknown. The [[A.T. Field]] appears to play some role in Angel metamorphoses. Taken together with what is said about the role of the A.T. Field elsewhere in the show, this implies that perhaps each Angel’s form is a tangible manifestation of its self-image. That is, where Lilin are more or less constrained to a common form, and must express their individuality in subtler ways, Adam’s children can simply be whatever they imagine themselves to be. The genetic pattern of [[Shamshel]] is reported to possess a 99.89% similarity to that of humans (''Homo sapiens''). While no comment is made on Shamshel’s brethren, this similarity can be deduced to apply universally to the group. In Sadamoto’s manga, the same percentage is applied to the Evas, implying – at least in Sadamoto’s view – the same genetic origins, which would be Adam. Of note here is that metamorphosis has no apparent effect on the Angels’ biological blueprints, indicating that it must operate on a more metaphysical level. ===Origins=== How Adam generated its offspring is completely unknown. Explanations can be as disparate as "pieces of Adam blown off at Second Impact grew into the various Angels" to "Adam laid the Angels' eggs billions of years ago, but didn't give them souls until the day of Second Impact, when they were scattered by the explosion." The proposed theories have varied pros and cons. Whether the Angels existed in some form prior to September 13, 2001, or only came into being on that very day, it doesn't seem to be any coincidence that Adam opened its Doors of Guf on Second Impact (implying the release of souls), and its children began to "appear" fifteen years later. See Also: '''[[Theory and Analysis:Angels' Origins|Angels' Origins]]'''. ===Motivations & Psychology=== The clues in the show itself regarding the Angels' intentions are varied and ambiguous. Kaji states in [[Episode 19]] that the Angels are trying to make contact with Adam, which would bring on a Third Impact which would destroy all mankind, and the behavior of some Angels is consistent with this statement. However, this gives rise to the question ''"Why were the Angels attacking Nerv H.Q. before Adam arrived there?"'' The Classified Information provided by the NGE2 game addresses this question: ''"Some of them were trying to access Lilith and reset all life, some of them had nothing in mind, and some were trying to recover their progenitor Adam."'' The revelation that Lilith was also a target explains the attacks on Nerv in the early episodes, and has become the most widely accepted explanation for this apparent contradiction. Prior to the publication of the C.I., the most widely accepted theory was that they were mistaking Lilith for Adam, as Kaworu seems to do in Ep. 24. It has also been suggested that Kaji's information is incorrect, and that the Angels were never after Adam. Adam's children are generally uncommunicative, and the forms they take tend to be inexpressive, with the result that we have little insight into their thoughts. However, some is provided by Leliel, Arael, and Armisael, who engage the Eva pilots in forms of communication. Some of the Angels also provide some insight into their psychology through their behavior, such as Sachiel and Zeruel. ===Common Traits=== While each of Adam’s children has a form unique to itself, there are many traits common to or prevalent among the group. (Some of these, in turn, can be found in other so-called "Angelic" lifeforms, such as the Seeds and Evas.) ====A.T. Field==== [[File:02 C270B big.jpg|thumb|200px|Sachiel's AT Field]] All of Adam’s children have [[soul]]s and, hence, A.T. Fields. Their A.T. Fields are much more powerful than those of LCL-based lifeforms, giving them various paranormal, especially [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychokinesis psychokinetic], abilities. Common applications include barriers ("force fields"), levitation, and metamorphosis. See Also: '''[[A.T. Field]]''' ====Blood==== Blood is observed (and quite a lot of it, when it splatters) in and from many of Adam’s children, including: Sachiel, Ramiel, Leliel, Bardiel, Zeruel, Arael, and Armisael. Their vital fluids are red in all instances save for Sachiel, whose is blue (albeit changed in Rebuild to adhere to the norm). It is unclear why, or how, Angels which manifest in such varied and bizarre forms still possess circulatory systems and blood. On one level, it might be considered a visceral reminder of their intrinsically human nature. In the Rebuild continuity, the Angels dissolve into a red fluid resembling blood when their cores are destroyed, and this is attributed to the collapse of their A.T. Fields.'''[[Evangelion 1.0: You Are (Not) Alone]]''':
*(observing the crater of "blood" left by the Fourth Angel's self-destruction) *Ritsuko: "The collapse of the Angel's A.T. Field was more destructive than we predicted." *Ibuki: "This blood is like a lake from hell... It reminds me of the Second Impact." This red fluid appears to be a form of [[LCL]]. '''See Also''': [[Image collapse]]. ====Blood Type==== Most register “Blood Type Blue.” “Orange” is seen on some rare occasions (Leliel's spherical shadow and Eva-03 possessed by Bardiel; Armisael cycles between orange and blue when it first appears). What “Blood Type” actually refers to however, is unclear. See Also: '''[[Blood Type]]''' ====Core==== [[File:OP C028 big.jpg|thumb|200px|An Angel's Core in the opening]] The [[core]] is a spherical red organ containing the soul and S² Engine. This is observed in nearly all of Adam’s children, and in Adam itself. See Also: '''[[Core]]''' ====Energy Projection==== Several Angels demonstrate an ability to harness energy to trigger explosions or generate focused-energy beams or energy-based projectiles. Angels possessing eyes – Sachiel, Israfel, and Zeruel – involve them in the energy projection process, although it's not entirely clear how this works. (I.e., if the eyes actually emit the energy, or if they simply act as a "focal point" of some sort.) Both Sachiel and Zeruel are capable of projecting cross-shaped explosions. Eva-01 and Eva-02 appear to have the same ability, suggesting that it perhaps "runs in the family." ====Halo==== [[File:Rebuild1 trailer2 C27 sachi-jump 1.jpg|thumb|200px|Fourth Angel's Halo]] In the Rebuild continuity, nearly all Angels are shown deploying a halo at some point, typically when using levitation powers. See Also: '''[[Halo]]''' ====Regeneration==== Most Angels are capable of enduring vast amounts of physical damage. As long as an Angel's core and S² Engine are intact, it can theoretically regenerate the damaged tissue, given enough time. ====S² Engine / Fruit of Life==== [[File:19 C356 eva01 meat.jpg|thumb|200px|Eva-01 devouring Zeruel's S² Organ, a S² Engine equivalent.]] Possessed by all of Adam’s children. The source of their theoretically limitless motive energy. Despite the name, the S² Engine appears to be organic in nature. See Also: '''[[S² Engine]]''' & '''[[Fruit of Life]]''' ==Angels in Rebuild (New Theatrical Edition)== The specific Angels appearing in the Rebuild or New Theatrical Edition continuity diverge from their original counterparts, beginning subtly in Evangelion 1.0 and proceeding in Evangelion 2.0 to the point that some Angels do not have any original counterparts. Furthermore, the identity of Rebuild's 11th Angel is a complete mystery. Note that with the exception of Lilith and Kaworu Nagisa, none of the Angels in the Rebuild continuity are known to have names, being known only by their ordinals. Referring to them by the names of their NGE equivalents or closest equivalents is a convention within the fandom employed for ease of communication, and they are likewise included in the list below with their corresponding NGE Angels, although the lack of names and growing divergence in visual appearance and abilities suggests the strong possibility that the Angels are '''not''' in fact, reimagined versions of the NGE designs but entirely different beings within the new continuity. ==List of Angels== This is a breakdown of all numbered Angels according to the three main ''Evangelion'' continuities. Seele is thought to obtain the Angel names and ordinals from the [[Dead Sea Scrolls|Secret Dead Sea Scrolls]]. It is unclear why entities such as the Evangelions (copies of the Seeds) and [[Rei Ayanami]] (Lilith's "answer to" Tabris) are not included, since they would seem to possess all of the required qualifications under the more expansive definition of "Angel." The reason may involve, in some way, the scenario that Seele are following in order to achieve Human Instrumentality. As noted above, Rebuild (NTE) Angels are listed alongside their closest NGE counterparts, even though they are not given names in the new continuity. {| border="1" cellpadding="5" cellspacing="0" |- ! width="16%" rowspan="2" | Thumbnail ! width="15%" rowspan="2" | Name ! width="40%" rowspan="2" | Description ! width="12%" colspan="3" | Ordinal |- ! width="6%" | NGE ! width="6%" | Manga ! width="6%" | Rebuild (NTE) Counterpart |- | [[Image:15_C280A_giant-of-light.jpg|175px]] | [[Adam]] | The first human on [[Earth]]. Progenitor of all "Angelic" Angels. | 1st | 1st | Unclear, multiple incarnations |- | [[Image:M25 Lilith Cross Ritsuko.jpg|175px]] | [[Lilith]] | Progenitor of mankind ([[Lilin]]) and most other organisms on Earth. | 2nd | 2nd | 2nd |- | [[Image:Eva2-22 C0026B crop.jpg|175px]] | [[Third Angel]] | Discovered in permafrost prior to Sachiel's attack. Fought [[Evangelion Unit-05]] at [[Bethany Base]] in ''[[Evangelion 2.0: You Can (Not) Advance|Evangelion 2.0]]''. | - | - | 3rd |- | [[ Image:Sachiel_Monitor.jpg|175px]] | [[Sachiel]] | First Angel to be encountered in the series. An amphibious humanoid Angel. | 3rd | 3rd | [[Fourth Angel|4th]] |- | [[Image:03_C208_shamshel-comp_crop.jpg|150px]] | [[Shamshel]] | Arthropod-like Angel with deadly "whips of light." | 4th | 4th | [[Fifth Angel|5th]] |- | [[Image:Ramiel_110.jpg|175px]] | [[Ramiel]] | Extremely powerful octahedral Angel with deadly particle beam and immensely strong A.T. Field. | 5th | 5th | [[Sixth Angel|6th]] |- | [[Image:Ep08_gaghiel.jpg|175px]] | [[Gaghiel]] | Aquatic Angel the size of an aircraft carrier. | 6th | 6th | - |- | [[Image:Eva2-22 C0124 ticktock.jpg|175px]] | [[Seventh Angel]] | An incredibly tall clock-like Angel. | - | - |7th |- | [[Image:09_C346_israfel-2jump.jpg|175px]] | [[Israfel]] | Angel capable of alternating between singular and "twin" forms. | 7th | 7th | - |- | [[Image:Ep10_sandalphon.jpg|175px]] | [[Sandalphon]] | Rapidly matured while still in embryonic stage in volcano. Capable of withstanding extremes of temperature and pressure. Resembles the Cambrian predator ''Anomalocaris''. | 8th | - | - |- | [[Image:11_C337_matarael.jpg|175px]] | [[Matarael]] | Four-legged spider-like Angel that secretes acid from its ventral eye. | 9th | - | - |- | [[Image:12_C250_sahaquiel.jpg|175px]] | [[Sahaquiel]] | Monstrous Angel that threatens to destroy Tokyo-3 by falling on the city from low earth orbit. | 10th | UnnumberedSadamoto skips from 7th Angel (Israfel) to 8th Angel (Bardiel), despite having the Evas sortie against Sahaquiel in between. | [[Eighth Angel|8th]] |- | [[Image:Ep13_ireul_pribnowbox.jpg|175px]] | [[Ireul]] | Microscopic Angel that appears deep inside Nerv Headquarters. Capable of hacking into computer systems. | 11th | - | - |- | [[Image:Ep16_leliel_through_binoculars.jpg|175px]] | [[Leliel]] | Angel with a black hole-like shadow body that exists in higher dimensions. | 12th | - | - |- | [[Image:18_C156_bardiel.jpg|175px]] | [[Bardiel]] | Has a physical structure similar to a slime fungus. Parasitizes and gains control over [[Evangelion Unit-03]]. | 13th | 8th | [[Ninth Angel|9th]] |- | [[Image:19_C160_zeruel.jpg|175px]] | [[Zeruel]] | Extremely powerful Angel with strong A.T. Field and no shortage of destructive power. Meets an especially brutal fate. | 14th | 9th | [[Tenth Angel|10th]] |- | [[Image:22_C275_arael.jpg|175px]] | [[Arael]] | Glowing suborbital bird-like Angel that probes minds with a beam of light. | 15th | 10th | - |- | [[Image:23_C061_armisael.jpg|175px]] | [[Armisael]] | First appears as a glowing double helix. Capable of changing form and fusing with an Eva. | 16th | 11th | - |- | [[Image:Eva3-33 C1259 crop.jpg|175px]] | [[Twelfth Angel]] | A polymorphous Angel reminiscent of Armisael. Trapped for fourteen years within [[Evangelion Mark.06]] under mysterious circumstances. Ultimately absorbed by [[Evangelion 13]]. | - | - | 12th |- | [[Image:24_C282_kaworu.jpg|175px]] | [[Tabris]] | Ostensibly a fifteen year old human male. Vessel of Adam's soul. | 17th | 12th | 13th/previously 1st |- | [[Image:M26 liveaction crowd.jpg|175px]] | [[Lilin]] | Humans – as we ''tend'' to think of them. | 18th | 13th | ? |} ==Angels in Rebuild== {{expand}} {{Rebuildcontentstart}} The Third through Thirteenth Angels in [[Rebuild of Evangelion|Rebuild]] are narratively equivalent to Adam's children. However, they have not yet had their origins divulged. No entity called "Adam" is known to exist in Rebuild, only four "''[[Adams]]''." It is likely that the Angels' origins are tied in some way to Second Impact, but the specifics are a complete unknown. {{Rebuildcontentend}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Angel List}} [[Category:A to Z]] [[Category:Angels]] [[Category:Religious and Esoteric References]]
html:
Angel (使徒, shito) is an ambiguous term that has various meanings within the Evangelion mythos depending on context.

Contents

  • 1 Usage
    • 1.1 Analysis
  • 2 Translation
  • 3 Adam's Children
    • 3.1 Nature
    • 3.2 Origins
    • 3.3 Motivations & Psychology
    • 3.4 Common Traits
      • 3.4.1 A.T. Field
      • 3.4.2 Blood
      • 3.4.3 Blood Type
      • 3.4.4 Core
      • 3.4.5 Energy Projection
      • 3.4.6 Halo
      • 3.4.7 Regeneration
      • 3.4.8 S² Engine / Fruit of Life
  • 4 Angels in Rebuild (New Theatrical Edition)
  • 5 List of Angels
  • 6 Angels in Rebuild
  • 7 References

Usage[edit]

As most commonly used in the show and elsewhere, the word "Angel" refers to any of Adam's fifteen children, whom Nerv must systematically destroy over the course of the story. Adam itself, the designated "First Angel," is occasionally included with its offspring when speaking of Angels collectively.[1]

However, considering Seele's list of designated "Angels" in full, it includes entities as disparate as the Seeds of Life (1st: Adam and 2nd: Lilith[2][3]), the natural progeny of Adam (3rd~16th), the human-shaped vessel of Adam's soul (17th: Kaworu Nagisa), and Homo sapiens itself (18th: Lilin). This conflict between the heavily-reinforced concept of "Angel" (god-like beings antithetical to human life) and the more inclusive version rightfully raises the question of what an Angel is actually intended to be. Evangelion plays a similar game with terms related to humanity (hito, ningen, and jinrui).

Analysis[edit]

One possibility is that "Angel" actually refers to all beings whose origin is ultimately traceable to the First Ancestral Race. This species, the prototypical humans of NGE, produced the Seeds of Life in order to propagate new lifeforms on distant worlds, into which the F.A.R.'s souls would be reincarnated.[4] Consider also that both "angel" and "apostle" (the literal meaning of Angel's Japanese equivalent, shito) inherently mean "messenger." Furthermore, the F.A.R. are stated to be esteemed as gods by Adam's children and the Lilin.[5] (On a perhaps related note, Rei muses, "What is a human? A creation of God? Is man a human creation?")[6] "Angels", then – which we know to refer to both Seeds of Life and their progeny – could be thought of as "messengers" for their "God(s)." The "message" to be disseminated is humanity itself.

Translation[edit]

"Making something... Nurturing something is really great. You can see and learn so many things from the process."
Please help improve this article or section by expanding it.
If you have questions on what to improve/expand, please discuss on the talk page.

The characters in NGE are actually calling the Angels "shito," which means "apostle," while the Japanese word normally used for the western concept of Angel is "tenshi." However, this isn't a translation mistake on the part of NGE's U.S. distributors. The "Angel" designation is clearly seen in the show's opening and at various times within the show itself, and the word "Angel" was retained in the English version of the show at the request of the show's Japanese creators[Source needed]. At one point in the show's production, the term "Apostolo" was going to be used as a counterpart to "shito" instead of "Angel.".[7]

Adam's Children[edit]

Adam’s children (アダムの子供達, Adamu no kodomotachi)[8] refers to the Angels originated from the Seed of Life Adam. In the Neon Genesis Evangelion TV series, Adam's children encompass the Third through Seventeenth Angels (manga: Third through Twelfth). The first thirteen are Adam’s natural offspring (upon whom this article will focus). Adam’s last child, Tabris, is the result of an experiment in which Lilin genes fused into Adam.

Adam's children began appearing around Tokyo-3 fifteen years after Second Impact, starting with the 3rd Angel, Sachiel. It is assumed that the Angels' intention is to cause Third Impact and reclaim Earth from Lilith's children (Homo sapiens). The Evangelions' task is to protect mankind by defeating Adam's children, in accordance with both Gendo Ikari's and Seele's plans.

Nature[edit]

"The story presented to the public was just a cover-up."
 The neutrality and accuracy of the article is disputed. It should be cleaned up to present unbiased information.
Please discuss this issue, or begin editing the page.


Despite being segregated into separate “Angels” (Third, Fourth, etc.), Adam’s children essentially comprise a single race of humanity – one possible variation upon a theme. (The Lilin are another.) They are described as “humans who cast aside human form.” Accordingly, they appear to begin life as humanoid entities, developing embryologically in perhaps the same way as Adam or an Eva would. Going by Sandalphon, the Angels develop inside oblong objects resembling eggs, compared in the show to a chrysalis. After developing to an indeterminate point[9], the Angel's human form is “rejected.” Immediately prior to hatching (again, going by Sandalphon's precedent), the Angel undergoes a spontaneous metamorphosis into its "adult form," then emerges from its "chrysalis."[10]

How the form is selected and its structure determined is entirely unknown. The A.T. Field appears to play some role in Angel metamorphoses. Taken together with what is said about the role of the A.T. Field elsewhere in the show, this implies that perhaps each Angel’s form is a tangible manifestation of its self-image. That is, where Lilin are more or less constrained to a common form, and must express their individuality in subtler ways, Adam’s children can simply be whatever they imagine themselves to be. The genetic pattern of Shamshel is reported to possess a 99.89% similarity to that of humans (Homo sapiens). While no comment is made on Shamshel’s brethren, this similarity can be deduced to apply universally to the group. In Sadamoto’s manga, the same percentage is applied to the Evas, implying – at least in Sadamoto’s view – the same genetic origins, which would be Adam. Of note here is that metamorphosis has no apparent effect on the Angels’ biological blueprints, indicating that it must operate on a more metaphysical level.

Origins[edit]

How Adam generated its offspring is completely unknown. Explanations can be as disparate as "pieces of Adam blown off at Second Impact grew into the various Angels" to "Adam laid the Angels' eggs billions of years ago, but didn't give them souls until the day of Second Impact, when they were scattered by the explosion." The proposed theories have varied pros and cons. Whether the Angels existed in some form prior to September 13, 2001, or only came into being on that very day, it doesn't seem to be any coincidence that Adam opened its Doors of Guf on Second Impact (implying the release of souls), and its children began to "appear" fifteen years later. See Also: Angels' Origins.

Motivations & Psychology[edit]

The clues in the show itself regarding the Angels' intentions are varied and ambiguous. Kaji states in Episode 19 that the Angels are trying to make contact with Adam, which would bring on a Third Impact which would destroy all mankind, and the behavior of some Angels is consistent with this statement. However, this gives rise to the question "Why were the Angels attacking Nerv H.Q. before Adam arrived there?" The Classified Information provided by the NGE2 game addresses this question:

"Some of them were trying to access Lilith and reset all life, some of them had nothing in mind, and some were trying to recover their progenitor Adam."

The revelation that Lilith was also a target explains the attacks on Nerv in the early episodes, and has become the most widely accepted explanation for this apparent contradiction.

Prior to the publication of the C.I., the most widely accepted theory was that they were mistaking Lilith for Adam, as Kaworu seems to do in Ep. 24. It has also been suggested that Kaji's information is incorrect, and that the Angels were never after Adam. Adam's children are generally uncommunicative, and the forms they take tend to be inexpressive, with the result that we have little insight into their thoughts. However, some is provided by Leliel, Arael, and Armisael, who engage the Eva pilots in forms of communication. Some of the Angels also provide some insight into their psychology through their behavior, such as Sachiel and Zeruel.

Common Traits[edit]

While each of Adam’s children has a form unique to itself, there are many traits common to or prevalent among the group. (Some of these, in turn, can be found in other so-called "Angelic" lifeforms, such as the Seeds and Evas.)

A.T. Field[edit]

All of Adam’s children have souls and, hence, A.T. Fields. Their A.T. Fields are much more powerful than those of LCL-based lifeforms, giving them various paranormal, especially psychokinetic, abilities. Common applications include barriers ("force fields"), levitation, and metamorphosis.

See Also: A.T. Field

Blood[edit]

Blood is observed (and quite a lot of it, when it splatters) in and from many of Adam’s children, including: Sachiel, Ramiel, Leliel, Bardiel, Zeruel, Arael, and Armisael. Their vital fluids are red in all instances save for Sachiel, whose is blue (albeit changed in Rebuild to adhere to the norm). It is unclear why, or how, Angels which manifest in such varied and bizarre forms still possess circulatory systems and blood. On one level, it might be considered a visceral reminder of their intrinsically human nature.

In the Rebuild continuity, the Angels dissolve into a red fluid resembling blood when their cores are destroyed, and this is attributed to the collapse of their A.T. Fields.[11] This red fluid appears to be a form of LCL. See Also: Image collapse.

Blood Type[edit]

Most register “Blood Type Blue.” “Orange” is seen on some rare occasions (Leliel's spherical shadow and Eva-03 possessed by Bardiel; Armisael cycles between orange and blue when it first appears). What “Blood Type” actually refers to however, is unclear.

See Also: Blood Type

Core[edit]

The core is a spherical red organ containing the soul and S² Engine. This is observed in nearly all of Adam’s children, and in Adam itself.

See Also: Core

Energy Projection[edit]

Several Angels demonstrate an ability to harness energy to trigger explosions or generate focused-energy beams or energy-based projectiles. Angels possessing eyes – Sachiel, Israfel, and Zeruel – involve them in the energy projection process, although it's not entirely clear how this works. (I.e., if the eyes actually emit the energy, or if they simply act as a "focal point" of some sort.)

Both Sachiel and Zeruel are capable of projecting cross-shaped explosions. Eva-01 and Eva-02 appear to have the same ability, suggesting that it perhaps "runs in the family."

Halo[edit]

In the Rebuild continuity, nearly all Angels are shown deploying a halo at some point, typically when using levitation powers. See Also: Halo

Regeneration[edit]

Most Angels are capable of enduring vast amounts of physical damage. As long as an Angel's core and S² Engine are intact, it can theoretically regenerate the damaged tissue, given enough time.

S² Engine / Fruit of Life[edit]

Possessed by all of Adam’s children. The source of their theoretically limitless motive energy. Despite the name, the S² Engine appears to be organic in nature. See Also: S² Engine & Fruit of Life

Angels in Rebuild (New Theatrical Edition)[edit]

The specific Angels appearing in the Rebuild or New Theatrical Edition continuity diverge from their original counterparts, beginning subtly in Evangelion 1.0 and proceeding in Evangelion 2.0 to the point that some Angels do not have any original counterparts. Furthermore, the identity of Rebuild's 11th Angel is a complete mystery.

Note that with the exception of Lilith and Kaworu Nagisa, none of the Angels in the Rebuild continuity are known to have names, being known only by their ordinals. Referring to them by the names of their NGE equivalents or closest equivalents is a convention within the fandom employed for ease of communication, and they are likewise included in the list below with their corresponding NGE Angels, although the lack of names and growing divergence in visual appearance and abilities suggests the strong possibility that the Angels are not in fact, reimagined versions of the NGE designs but entirely different beings within the new continuity.

List of Angels[edit]

This is a breakdown of all numbered Angels according to the three main Evangelion continuities. Seele is thought to obtain the Angel names and ordinals from the Secret Dead Sea Scrolls. It is unclear why entities such as the Evangelions (copies of the Seeds) and Rei Ayanami (Lilith's "answer to" Tabris) are not included, since they would seem to possess all of the required qualifications under the more expansive definition of "Angel." The reason may involve, in some way, the scenario that Seele are following in order to achieve Human Instrumentality. As noted above, Rebuild (NTE) Angels are listed alongside their closest NGE counterparts, even though they are not given names in the new continuity.

Thumbnail Name Description Ordinal
NGE Manga Rebuild (NTE) Counterpart
Adam The first human on Earth. Progenitor of all "Angelic" Angels. 1st 1st Unclear, multiple incarnations
Lilith Progenitor of mankind (Lilin) and most other organisms on Earth. 2nd 2nd 2nd
Third Angel Discovered in permafrost prior to Sachiel's attack. Fought Evangelion Unit-05 at Bethany Base in Evangelion 2.0. - - 3rd
Sachiel First Angel to be encountered in the series. An amphibious humanoid Angel. 3rd 3rd 4th
Shamshel Arthropod-like Angel with deadly "whips of light." 4th 4th 5th
Ramiel Extremely powerful octahedral Angel with deadly particle beam and immensely strong A.T. Field. 5th 5th 6th
Gaghiel Aquatic Angel the size of an aircraft carrier. 6th 6th -
Seventh Angel An incredibly tall clock-like Angel. - - 7th
Israfel Angel capable of alternating between singular and "twin" forms. 7th 7th -
Sandalphon Rapidly matured while still in embryonic stage in volcano. Capable of withstanding extremes of temperature and pressure. Resembles the Cambrian predator Anomalocaris. 8th - -
Matarael Four-legged spider-like Angel that secretes acid from its ventral eye. 9th - -
Sahaquiel Monstrous Angel that threatens to destroy Tokyo-3 by falling on the city from low earth orbit. 10th Unnumbered[12] 8th
Ireul Microscopic Angel that appears deep inside Nerv Headquarters. Capable of hacking into computer systems. 11th - -
Leliel Angel with a black hole-like shadow body that exists in higher dimensions. 12th - -
Bardiel Has a physical structure similar to a slime fungus. Parasitizes and gains control over Evangelion Unit-03. 13th 8th 9th
Zeruel Extremely powerful Angel with strong A.T. Field and no shortage of destructive power. Meets an especially brutal fate. 14th 9th 10th
Arael Glowing suborbital bird-like Angel that probes minds with a beam of light. 15th 10th -
Armisael First appears as a glowing double helix. Capable of changing form and fusing with an Eva. 16th 11th -
Twelfth Angel A polymorphous Angel reminiscent of Armisael. Trapped for fourteen years within Evangelion Mark.06 under mysterious circumstances. Ultimately absorbed by Evangelion 13. - - 12th
Tabris Ostensibly a fifteen year old human male. Vessel of Adam's soul. 17th 12th 13th/previously 1st
Lilin Humans – as we tend to think of them. 18th 13th ?

Angels in Rebuild[edit]

"Making something... Nurturing something is really great. You can see and learn so many things from the process."
Please help improve this article or section by expanding it.
If you have questions on what to improve/expand, please discuss on the talk page.

The Third through Thirteenth Angels in Rebuild are narratively equivalent to Adam's children. However, they have not yet had their origins divulged. No entity called "Adam" is known to exist in Rebuild, only four "Adams." It is likely that the Angels' origins are tied in some way to Second Impact, but the specifics are a complete unknown.

References[edit]

  1. ↑ Episode 12:
    • Misato: "Destroying Angels is my duty."
      Ritsuko: "Your duty? Don't make me laugh. This is for your own sake, isn't it? Your revenge against the Angels!"
    • Misato: "The only thing that was clear to me was I wanted to destroy the Angels who caused the Second Impact. So in order to do that, I joined Nerv."
  2. ↑ Lilith's designation as the Second Angel is never provided in-show, although it can be reasonably deduced due to the fact that no Second Angel is ever named, and Lilith is never assigned an ordinal. Its status as Second Angel is stated outright in several supplemental canonical sources (including Bandai's collectible card game, the Classified Information, and Evangelion Chronicle). It is also stated directly in the Rebuild canon:
    Evangelion 1.0: You Are (Not) Alone. Misato (showing Shinji the giant in Central Dogma):
    "[This is] the bringer of life to this planet, and the agent of its extinction. The Second Angel, Lilith."
  3. ↑ Episode 26'. Misato: "Shinji-kun, we humans were born from the one called Lilith---a source of life, just like Adam. We are the 18th Angel." This translation stems from Bochan_bird's reading of the script, which is ambiguous as spoken; he apparently also ran the question by Tsurumaki in 2001.
  4. ↑ Neon Genesis Evangelion 2: Another Cases. Kaworu's Scenario: Good Ending.
  5. ↑ Neon Genesis Evangelion 2. Classified Information. First Ancestral Race.
  6. ↑ Neon Genesis Evangelion. Episode 14. Platinum subtitles.
  7. ↑ Omori: Yes, it is. It also has challenges that seem to anticipate cyberpunk. However, Mitsuse's world is more oriental in its view of impermanence, but Evangelion is more Western.
    Anno: I'm not into Western civilization, you know. Somehow I don't trust Western civilization very much.
    Omori: Is that as something to be denied?
    Anno: It doesn't relate to me, so I can use it. If I were a Christian, I'd be too scared to use Christian stuffs.
    Omori: Indeed. You don't have any attachments, so you can use the name of an angel. Like let's use this name because its sense of language sounds nice.
    Anno: To equate apostles and angels is so much to complain about from a westerner's point of view. There is an American worker in our company, and he scolded me for many things, saying it was wrong. That's normal. But I didn't care about that and just did it. - The World of Neon Genesis Evangelion, SF Magazine, Aug 1996
  8. ↑ The collective grouping has no unique canonical name, although the phrase “Adam’s children” was used by Misato in a preliminary draft for Episode 25’.
  9. ↑ In episode 10, Ritsuko says that Sandalphon hatched sooner than expected, leaving the “norm” unknown.
  10. ↑ While the terms used invite an insect analogy, there is no canonical evidence that the Angels had a free-moving "pupal" stage at some point and later cocooned themselves. Rather, "chrysalis" seems to apply to the extent that the entity it contains undergoes a drastic metamorphosis before emerging.
  11. ↑ Evangelion 1.0: You Are (Not) Alone:
    • (observing the crater of "blood" left by the Fourth Angel's self-destruction)
    • Ritsuko: "The collapse of the Angel's A.T. Field was more destructive than we predicted."
    • Ibuki: "This blood is like a lake from hell... It reminds me of the Second Impact."
  12. ↑ Sadamoto skips from 7th Angel (Israfel) to 8th Angel (Bardiel), despite having the Evas sortie against Sahaquiel in between.

Sachiel | Shamshel | Ramiel | Gaghiel | Israfel | Sandalphon | Matarael | Sahaquiel

chunks.content:
thumb|right|"Angel face."

Angel (使徒, shito) is an ambiguous term that has various meanings within the Evangelion mythos depending on context.
Usage
As most commonly used in the show and elsewhere, the word "Angel" refers to any of Adam's fifteen children, whom Nerv must systematically destroy over the course of the story. Adam itself, the designated "First Angel," is occasionally included with its offspring when speaking of Angels collectively.Episode 12: 
 Misato: "Destroying Angels is my duty."  Ritsuko: "Your duty? Don't make me laugh. This is for your own sake, isn't it? Your revenge against the Angels!"  
 Misato: "The only thing that was clear to me was I wanted to destroy the Angels who caused the Second Impact. So in order to do that, I joined Nerv." 

However, considering Seele's list of designated "Angels" in full, it includes entities as disparate as the Seeds of Life (1st: Adam and 2nd: LilithLilith's designation as the Second Angel is never provided in-show, although it can be reasonably deduced due to the fact that no Second Angel is ever named, and Lilith is never assigned an ordinal. Its status as Second Angel is stated outright in several supplemental canonical sources (including Bandai's collectible card game, the Classified Information, and Evangelion Chronicle). It is also stated directly in the Rebuild canon:
Evangelion 1.0: You Are (Not) Alone. Misato (showing Shinji the giant in Central Dogma): 
"[This is] the bringer of life to this planet, and the agent of its extinction. The Second Angel, Lilith."Episode 26'. Misato: "Shinji-kun, we humans were born from the one called Lilith---a source of life, just like Adam. We are the 18th Angel." This translation stems from Bochan_bird's reading of the script, which is ambiguous as spoken; he apparently also ran the question by Tsurumaki in 2001.), the natural progeny of Adam (3rd~16th), the human-shaped vessel of Adam's soul (17th: Kaworu Nagisa), and Homo sapiens itself (18th: Lilin).
This conflict between the heavily-reinforced concept of "Angel" (god-like beings antithetical to human life) and the more inclusive version

(17th: Kaworu Nagisa), and Homo sapiens itself (18th: Lilin).
This conflict between the heavily-reinforced concept of "Angel" (god-like beings antithetical to human life) and the more inclusive version rightfully raises the question of what an Angel is actually intended to be. Evangelion plays a similar game with terms related to humanity (hito, ningen, and jinrui).
Analysis
One possibility is that "Angel" actually refers to all beings whose origin is ultimately traceable to the First Ancestral Race. This species, the prototypical humans of NGE, produced the Seeds of Life in order to propagate new lifeforms on distant worlds, into which the F.A.R.'s souls would be reincarnated.Neon Genesis Evangelion 2: Another Cases. Kaworu's Scenario: Good Ending. Consider also that both "angel" and "apostle" (the literal meaning of Angel's Japanese equivalent, shito) inherently mean "messenger." Furthermore, the F.A.R. are stated to be esteemed as gods by Adam's children and the Lilin.Neon Genesis Evangelion 2. Classified Information. First Ancestral Race. (On a perhaps related note, Rei muses, "What is a human? A creation of God? Is man a human creation?")Neon Genesis Evangelion. Episode 14. Platinum subtitles. "Angels", then – which we know to refer to both Seeds of Life and their progeny – could be thought of as "messengers" for their "God(s)." The "message" to be disseminated is humanity itself.

Translation

The characters in NGE are actually calling the Angels "shito," which means "apostle," while the Japanese word normally used for the western concept of Angel is "tenshi."  However, this isn't a translation mistake on the part of NGE's U.S. distributors. The "Angel" designation is clearly seen in the show's opening and at various times within the show itself, and the word "Angel" was retained in the English version of the show at the request of the show's Japanese creators.
At one point in the show's production, the term "Apostolo" was going to be used as a counterpart to "shito" instead of "Angel.".Omori: Yes, it is. It also has challenges that seem to anticipate cyberpunk. However, Mitsuse's world is more oriental in its view

was going to be used as a counterpart to "shito" instead of "Angel.".Omori: Yes, it is. It also has challenges that seem to anticipate cyberpunk. However, Mitsuse's world is more oriental in its view of impermanence, but Evangelion is more Western.Anno: I'm not into Western civilization, you know. Somehow I don't trust Western civilization very much.Omori: Is that as something to be denied?Anno: It doesn't relate to me, so I can use it. If I were a Christian, I'd be too scared to use Christian stuffs.Omori: Indeed. You don't have any attachments, so you can use the name of an angel. Like let's use this name because its sense of language sounds nice.Anno: To equate apostles and angels is so much to complain about from a westerner's point of view. There is an American worker in our company, and he scolded me for many things, saying it was wrong. That's normal. But I didn't care about that and just did it. - The World of Neon Genesis Evangelion, SF Magazine, Aug 1996

Adam's Children
Adam’s children (アダムの子供達, Adamu no kodomotachi)The collective grouping has no unique canonical name, although the phrase “Adam’s children” was used by Misato in a preliminary draft for Episode 25’. refers to the Angels originated from the Seed of Life Adam.
In the Neon Genesis Evangelion TV series, Adam's children encompass the Third through Seventeenth Angels (manga: Third through Twelfth). The first thirteen are Adam’s natural offspring (upon whom this article will focus). Adam’s last child, Tabris, is the result of an experiment in which Lilin genes fused into Adam.

Adam's children began appearing around Tokyo-3 fifteen years after Second Impact, starting with the 3rd Angel, Sachiel. It is assumed that the Angels' intention is to cause Third Impact and reclaim Earth from Lilith's children (Homo sapiens). The Evangelions' task is to protect mankind by defeating Adam's children, in accordance with both Gendo Ikari's and Seele's plans.
Nature

Despite being segregated into separate “Angels” (Third, Fourth, etc.), Adam’s children essentially comprise a single race of humanity – one possible variation upon a theme. (The Lilin are another.) They are described as “hum

.
Nature

Despite being segregated into separate “Angels” (Third, Fourth, etc.), Adam’s children essentially comprise a single race of humanity – one possible variation upon a theme. (The Lilin are another.) They are described as “humans who cast aside human form.” Accordingly, they appear to begin life as humanoid entities, developing embryologically in perhaps the same way as Adam or an Eva would. Going by Sandalphon, the Angels develop inside oblong objects resembling eggs, compared in the show to a chrysalis. After developing to an indeterminate pointIn episode 10, Ritsuko says that Sandalphon hatched sooner than expected, leaving the “norm” unknown., the Angel's human form is “rejected.” Immediately prior to hatching (again, going by Sandalphon's precedent), the Angel undergoes a spontaneous metamorphosis into its "adult form," then emerges from its "chrysalis." While the terms used invite an insect analogy, there is no canonical evidence that the Angels had a free-moving "pupal" stage at some point and later cocooned themselves. Rather, "chrysalis" seems to apply to the extent that the entity it contains undergoes a drastic metamorphosis before emerging.

How the form is selected and its structure determined is entirely unknown. The A.T. Field appears to play some role in Angel metamorphoses. Taken together with what is said about the role of the A.T. Field elsewhere in the show, this implies that perhaps each Angel’s form is a tangible manifestation of its self-image. That is, where Lilin are more or less constrained to a common form, and must express their individuality in subtler ways, Adam’s children can simply be whatever they imagine themselves to be.
The genetic pattern of Shamshel is reported to possess a 99.89% similarity to that of humans (Homo sapiens). While no comment is made on Shamshel’s brethren, this similarity can be deduced to apply universally to the group. In Sadamoto’s manga, the same percentage is applied to the Evas, implying – at least in Sadamoto’s view – the same genetic origins, which would be Adam. Of note here is that metamorphosis has no apparent effect on the Angels’ biological blueprints, indicating that it must operate on a more metaphysical level.

Origins
How Adam generated its offspring is completely unknown. Explanations can be as disparate as "pieces of Adam blown off at

no apparent effect on the Angels’ biological blueprints, indicating that it must operate on a more metaphysical level.

Origins
How Adam generated its offspring is completely unknown. Explanations can be as disparate as "pieces of Adam blown off at Second Impact grew into the various Angels" to "Adam laid the Angels' eggs billions of years ago, but didn't give them souls until the day of Second Impact, when they were scattered by the explosion." The proposed theories have varied pros and cons.
Whether the Angels existed in some form prior to September 13, 2001, or only came into being on that very day, it doesn't seem to be any coincidence that Adam opened its Doors of Guf on Second Impact (implying the release of souls), and its children began to "appear" fifteen years later.
See Also: Angels' Origins.

Motivations & Psychology
The clues in the show itself regarding the Angels' intentions are varied and ambiguous. Kaji states in Episode 19 that the Angels are trying to make contact with Adam, which would bring on a Third Impact which would destroy all mankind, and the behavior of some Angels is consistent with this statement.  However, this gives rise to the question "Why were the Angels attacking Nerv H.Q. before Adam arrived there?"  The Classified Information provided by the NGE2 game addresses this question:

"Some of them were trying to access Lilith and reset all life, some of them had nothing in mind, and some were trying to recover their progenitor Adam."  

The revelation that Lilith was also a target explains the attacks on Nerv in the early episodes, and has become the most widely accepted explanation for this apparent contradiction.

Prior to the publication of the C.I., the most widely accepted theory was that they were mistaking Lilith for Adam, as Kaworu seems to do in Ep. 24. It has also been suggested that Kaji's information is incorrect, and that the Angels were never after Adam. 
Adam's children are generally uncommunicative, and the forms they take tend to be inexpressive, with the result that we have little insight into their thoughts. However, some is provided by Leliel, Arael, and Armisael, who engage the Eva pilots in forms of communication. Some of the Angels also provide some insight into their psychology through their behavior, such as Sachiel and Zeruel.

Common Traits
While each of Adam’s children has a form unique to itself, there are many traits

pilots in forms of communication. Some of the Angels also provide some insight into their psychology through their behavior, such as Sachiel and Zeruel.

Common Traits
While each of Adam’s children has a form unique to itself, there are many traits common to or prevalent among the group. (Some of these, in turn, can be found in other so-called "Angelic" lifeforms, such as the Seeds and Evas.)

A.T. Field
thumb|200px|Sachiel's AT Field

All of Adam’s children have souls and, hence, A.T. Fields. Their A.T. Fields are much more powerful than those of LCL-based lifeforms, giving them various paranormal, especially psychokinetic, abilities. Common applications include barriers ("force fields"), levitation, and metamorphosis.

See Also: A.T. Field

Blood
Blood is observed (and quite a lot of it, when it splatters) in and from many of Adam’s children, including: Sachiel, Ramiel, Leliel, Bardiel, Zeruel, Arael, and Armisael. Their vital fluids are red in all instances save for Sachiel, whose is blue (albeit changed in Rebuild to adhere to the norm). It is unclear why, or how, Angels which manifest in such varied and bizarre forms still possess circulatory systems and blood. On one level, it might be considered a visceral reminder of their intrinsically human nature.

In the Rebuild continuity, the Angels dissolve into a red fluid resembling blood when their cores are destroyed, and this is attributed to the collapse of their A.T. Fields.Evangelion 1.0: You Are (Not) Alone:
(observing the crater of "blood" left by the Fourth Angel's self-destruction)
Ritsuko: "The collapse of the Angel's A.T. Field was more destructive than we predicted."
Ibuki: "This blood is like a lake from hell... It reminds me of the Second Impact." This red fluid appears to be a form of LCL.
See Also: Image collapse.

Blood Type
Most register “Blood Type Blue.” “Orange” is seen on some rare occasions (Leliel's spherical shadow and Eva-03 possessed by Bardiel; Armisael cycles between orange and blue when it first appears). What “Blood Type” actually refers to however, is unclear.

See Also: Blood Type

Core
thumb|200px|An Angel's Core in

03 possessed by Bardiel; Armisael cycles between orange and blue when it first appears). What “Blood Type” actually refers to however, is unclear.

See Also: Blood Type

Core
thumb|200px|An Angel's Core in the opening

The core is a spherical red organ containing the soul and S² Engine. This is observed in nearly all of Adam’s children, and in Adam itself.

See Also: Core

Energy Projection
Several Angels demonstrate an ability to harness energy to trigger explosions or generate focused-energy beams or energy-based projectiles. Angels possessing eyes – Sachiel, Israfel, and Zeruel – involve them in the energy projection process, although it's not entirely clear how this works. (I.e., if the eyes actually emit the energy, or if they simply act as a "focal point" of some sort.)

Both Sachiel and Zeruel are capable of projecting cross-shaped explosions. Eva-01 and Eva-02 appear to have the same ability, suggesting that it perhaps "runs in the family." 

Halo
thumb|200px|Fourth Angel's Halo

In the Rebuild continuity, nearly all Angels are shown deploying a halo at some point, typically when using levitation powers.
See Also: Halo

Regeneration
Most Angels are capable of enduring vast amounts of physical damage. As long as an Angel's core and S² Engine are intact, it can theoretically regenerate the damaged tissue, given enough time.

S² Engine / Fruit of Life
thumb|200px|Eva-01 devouring Zeruel's S² Organ, a S² Engine equivalent.

Possessed by all of Adam’s children. The source of their theoretically limitless motive energy. Despite the name, the S² Engine appears to be organic in nature.
See Also: S² Engine & Fruit of Life

Angels in Rebuild (New Theatrical Edition)
The specific Angels appearing in the Rebuild or New Theatrical Edition continuity diverge from their original counterparts, beginning subtly in Evangelion 1.0 and proceeding in Evangelion 2.0 to the point that some Angels do not have any original counterparts. Furthermore, the identity of Rebuild's 11th Angel is a complete mystery.

Note that with the exception of Lilith and Kaworu Nagisa, none of the Angels in the Rebuild continuity are known to have names, being known only by their ordinals. Referring to them by the names of their NGE equivalents or closest equivalents is a

exception of Lilith and Kaworu Nagisa, none of the Angels in the Rebuild continuity are known to have names, being known only by their ordinals. Referring to them by the names of their NGE equivalents or closest equivalents is a convention within the fandom employed for ease of communication, and they are likewise included in the list below with their corresponding NGE Angels, although the lack of names and growing divergence in visual appearance and abilities suggests the strong possibility that the Angels are not in fact, reimagined versions of the NGE designs but entirely different beings within the new continuity.

List of Angels
This is a breakdown of all numbered Angels according to the three main Evangelion continuities.
Seele is thought to obtain the Angel names and ordinals from the Secret Dead Sea Scrolls. It is unclear why entities such as the Evangelions (copies of the Seeds) and Rei Ayanami (Lilith's "answer to" Tabris) are not included, since they would seem to possess all of the required qualifications under the more expansive definition of "Angel." The reason may involve, in some way, the scenario that Seele are following in order to achieve Human Instrumentality.
As noted above, Rebuild (NTE) Angels are listed alongside their closest NGE counterparts, even though they are not given names in the new continuity.

Angels in Rebuild

The Third through Thirteenth Angels in Rebuild are narratively equivalent to Adam's children. However, they have not yet had their origins divulged. No entity called "Adam" is known to exist in Rebuild, only four "Adams." It is likely that the Angels' origins are tied in some way to Second Impact, but the specifics are a complete unknown.
